</edmx:Edmx>

Now multiply this by 500+ entities. That's your $metadata file. Finding "which fields on EmpJob are required and creatable via API" means scrolling through thousands of lines, cross-referencing Nullable="false" with sap:creatable="true", and manually building a spreadsheet.

I've done this. You've done this. We've all done this. And it takes hours.

The Solution: Upload, Ask, Done

Here's the new workflow. It takes three steps.

Step 1: Download Your $metadata

Navigate to your instance's metadata endpoint and save the XML:

 
 
https://your-instance.successfactors.com/odata/v2/$metadata

Save as metadata.xml. This file is typically 5–15 MB depending on your instance configuration.

Step 2: Upload to Claude and Ask

Open Claude (claude.ai or Claude Code) and upload the file. Then just ask for what you need in plain English:

"Parse this SuccessFactors metadata and create an Excel data dictionary with a summary sheet, individual sheets for each Employee Central entity, and a master list of all fields."

Step 3: Get Your Data Dictionary

In about 30 seconds, Claude generates a professional multi-sheet Excel workbook. Here's what each sheet looks like:

What You Get: The Excel Output

Summary Sheet

The first sheet gives you a bird's-eye view of your entire instance:

📊Your SF Data Dictionary is Complete!

Here's what I created for you:

Workbook Contents:

  1. Legend (Quick Reference)
    • Color coding guide
    • Column definitions and what they mean
    • Helps you understand the data at a glance
  2. Summary (Executive Overview)
    • 726 total entities
    • 13,469 total fields across all entities
    • 3,965 associations (relationships between entities)
    • 82 complex types
    • Breakdown by category (Employee Central, Position Management, Compensation, etc.)
  3. All Fields (Master Data Dictionary)
    • Every single field from every entity
    • Sortable and filterable
    • Includes: field name, type, required status, max length, labels, creatable/updatable flags
    • Color-coded for quick identification:
      • Yellow = Key fields (primary identifiers)
      • Light Red = Required fields (must have a value)
      • Light Blue = Custom fields (created via MDF)
  4. Entity-Specific Sheets (Top 30 Entities)
    • Detailed breakdown for the most important entities
    • Includes navigation properties
    • Ready for deep-dive analysis
  5. Navigation Properties (3,529 Relationships)
    • Shows how entities connect to each other
    • From/To entity mappings
    • Essential for understanding data flow and integration points

Use This For:

  • 📋Data Mapping — Connect SF fields to your payroll, ATS, or other systems
  • 🔍Field Discovery — Quickly find what fields exist and what they do
  • 📖Compliance & Documentation — Audit-ready reference for your instance
  • 🔗Integration Planning — Understand relationships between entities
  • 👥Team Communication — Non-technical stakeholders can understand the schema

You can now download, share, and use this in Excel with full filtering, sorting, and pivot table capabilities!

Real-World Use Cases

Let me walk through five scenarios I've personally used this tool on.

Use Case 1: New Implementation Data Dictionary

The ask: "We need a complete data dictionary for our Employee Central go-live documentation."

The prompt:

"Parse this metadata and create an Excel workbook. I need a summary sheet with all entities grouped by SF module, and detailed field sheets for: User, EmpEmployment, EmpJob, EmpCompensation, PerPersonal, PerEmail, PerPhone, PerNationalId, and PerAddressDEFLT. Also include a navigation properties sheet."

Time to deliver: 30 seconds instead of 2–3 days of manual work.

What I used to do: Open the metadata XML in Notepad++, Ctrl+F for each entity, manually copy field names into Excel, cross-reference attributes one by one. For a typical EC implementation with 10 core entities, this was a solid 2-day task.

Use Case 2: Integration Field Mapping

The ask: "Map all creatable fields on EmpJob to our payroll system — we need field name, label, type, and max length."

The prompt:

"From this metadata, create a CSV with all fields on EmpJob where sap:creatable is true. Include columns: Field Name, Label, Data Type, Max Length, Required. Sort by required fields first."

The result: A clean CSV file that the integration team can immediately import into their mapping tool. No manual parsing needed.

Use Case 3: Dev vs. Prod Comparison

This one is my favorite. After a series of transports, the client wanted to verify that their Production instance had all the same custom fields as Development.

The prompt:

"I've uploaded two metadata files — metadata_dev.xml and metadata_prod.xml. Compare them and show me: entities that exist in one but not the other, and custom fields (cust_) that differ between instances."*

The output: A comparison report showing:

  • 3 custom MDF objects existed in DEV but hadn't been transported to PROD
  • 7 custom fields on EmpJob were in PROD but missing from DEV (someone configured directly in Prod — a compliance issue!)
  • 2 fields had different Nullable settings between environments

This kind of configuration drift is a real problem in SF implementations, and this tool catches it instantly.

Use Case 4: Security Audit — "What Can This API User Access?"

The ask: "For our security review, we need to document every field that is filterable and sortable across all entities — these represent potential data extraction vectors."

The prompt:

"Parse this metadata and create an Excel sheet showing all fields across all entities where sap:filterable = true. Group by entity, and add a column showing whether the field contains PII (look for fields with labels containing 'name', 'email', 'phone', 'address', 'national', 'birth', 'gender', 'salary')."

This delivers a ready-made appendix for your security audit documentation.

Use Case 5: Quick Lookup During a Call

Not every use case needs a full export. Sometimes you're on a call with a client and they ask: "Is the businessUnit field on EmpJob required? Can we update it via API?"

The prompt:

"From this metadata, tell me about the businessUnit field on EmpJob — is it required, creatable, updatable, and what's the max length?"

Claude's response:

The businessUnit field on EmpJob is:

  • Type: Text (Edm.String), Max Length 128
  • Required: No (Nullable = true)
  • Creatable: Yes
  • Updatable: Yes
  • Filterable: Yes
  • Label: "Business Unit"

Answer delivered in 5 seconds while you're still on the call.

How to Get Started

Option 1: Use It Right Now in Claude.ai

  1. Go to claude.ai,
  2. Follow instructions provided on GitHub.
  3. Upload your $metadata XML file (download from your instance)
  4. Type your request: "Parse this SuccessFactors metadata into an Excel data dictionary"
  5. Download the generated Excel file

That's it. No installation, no configuration, no code.

Option 2: Install as a Claude Code Plugin

If you use Claude Code (Anthropic's terminal-based coding assistant), you can install this as a permanent plugin:

 

bash
/plugin install shaktibarath/sf-metadata-parser

Once installed, Claude automatically recognizes when you're working with SF metadata and applies the skill.

Option 3: Use via the Claude API

For automated pipelines (e.g., nightly metadata snapshots), you can call the skill programmatically via the Claude API with the Skills endpoint.

How to Download Your $metadata

If you haven't done this before, here's where to find it:

  1. URL format: https://<api-server>/odata/v2/$metadata
  2. Authentication: Use your SF admin credentials (Basic Auth) or an API user
  3. Example API servers by data center:
    • DC2: api2.successfactors.eu
    • DC4: api4.successfactors.com
    • DC8: api8.successfactors.com
    • DC10: api10.successfactors.com
    • DC12: api12.successfactors.com
    • DC55: api55.successfactors.eu
  4. Save as: Right-click → Save As → metadata.xml

Or use the browser developer tools (Network tab) if your instance requires SSO.

For a filtered metadata (specific entities only), append ?$filter= but note that the full metadata is more useful for comprehensive documentation.

What's Next

This tool is open-source on GitHub and I'm actively developing it. If you're an SF consultant, admin, or integration developer — I'd love your feedback. What outputs would save you the most time? What's missing?

Try It Now

The tool is available today:

  • Claude.ai — Upload your metadata XML and start asking questions
  • GitHub — https://github.com/shaktibarath/sf-metadata-parser
  • Claude Code — /plugin install shaktibarath/sf-metadata-parser

If it saves you even one afternoon of manual XML parsing, it was worth building.

Have questions or feedback? Find me on LinkedIn or open an issue on GitHub.

entry.title: Connect SAP OData to Your BPA Excel Forms
entry.content:
Introduction: You're using BPA (SAP Build Process Automation), and users need to fill out Excel files with SAP data. But here's the kicker - to create just one cost center, they have to manually fill 24 rows!
For 10 cost centers? That's  240 rows of mind-numbing data entry! They can't remember all these codes and  this takes more time

Instead of manual data entry, why not connect Excel directly to your SAP OData services? Give them dropdown menus filled with real, live data from your backend!
Prerequisites:
  • OData service
  • SAP system access
  • Excel
Step 1: Open the Gateway to Your Data
Fire up Excel and navigate to: Data → Get Data → From Other Sources → From OData Feed

Step 2: Set Up Your Connection 
When the OData feed dialog opens:
1. Click Advanced 
2. Click the dropdown and select New Parameter

Step 3: Create Your Parameter 
In the parameter setup:
Name: Give it something meaningful like "SAP_System"
Description: SAP System Connection
Type: Select "Any" for flexibility
Suggested Values: Choose "List of values"
 
Add your systems:
  • URL: Your OData service URL
  • Default Value: Your primary system
  • Current Value: What you want to use now

Note:  If you only have one system, skip the advanced stuff and just paste your URL in basic mode

Step 4: Point to Your OData Service
Enter your OData service URL and click OK
This is like giving Excel the address to your data 

Step 5: Authentication Time 
Excel will ask for your SAP credentials:
1. Enter your SAP username and password
2. Select the complete URL you provided
3. Click OK

Step 6: Choose Your Data 
You'll see a screen with checkboxes and multiple tables:
1. Check the boxes for tables you need
2. Look at the Load dropdown at the bottom
3. Select Load to

:377993i0B62606573A70E14:

Step 7: Import Data
1. Select Table
2. Click OK

Each table gets its own worksheet - Excel sheet

Step 8: The Dropdown Data Validation
  1. Select the cell where you want a dropdown
  2. Go to Data → Data Validation

Step 9: Configure Your Dropdown
In the Data Validation screen:
1. Choose List from the dropdown
2. For Source, click the sheet icon
3. Select your data range by selecting the column : `=SheetName!$A:$A`

Step 10: Test Your Creation
Click on your cell and a dropdown appears with real SAP data!

Automatic Data Refresh

To make sure users always see the latest SAP data:

  1. Right‑click on the Query
  2. Select Properties
  3. Enable the checkbox:
    “Refresh data when opening the file”

Now, every time a user opens the Excel file, the data refreshes automatically using their SAP login credentials.

No manual refresh needed. No outdated data. No security risk.

Maintaining SAP System Credentials in Excel (Without Sharing Your Password)

When working with SAP-connected Excel files, one common concern is:
“How do I share this file with users without exposing my SAP username and password?” SAP & Excel already have a secure way to handle this.

How SAP Credentials Are Handled

When you configure an SAP OData connection in Excel (like in Step 3 of your process), it’s natural to wonder whether the file stores your username and password.
But don’t worry — you should never save your credentials inside the file.

Instead, when you’re preparing a template for users (whether through BPA forms, SharePoint, or email), follow this approach:

How to Prepare the Template Securely
  1. You connect the file to the SAP system using your own credentials.
  2. After testing, go to:
    Data → Get Data → Data Source Settings → Edit Permissions → Edit → Basic - remove your username and password 
  3. Save the settings and close the file.

Now, when another user opens the same Excel template:

They will be prompted to enter their own SAP username and password

(Step 5)

This means:

  • You don’t share your credentials
  • They authenticate using their SAP login
  • They only see the data allowed by their SAP roles

Perfectly safe — and no password is stored in the file.

Benefits
  • User-friendly
  • Error reduction
  • Time savings

Conclusion : Users struggle with massive Excel data entry. You've just built a bridge between user-friendly Excel and powerful SAP backends. OData connections turn Excel into a smart frontend and Dropdowns eliminate user errors.

entry.title: Step-by-Step: Calling S/4HANA Private Cloud Standard OData APIs with OAuth 2.0 from Client Tool
entry.content:
Introduction

When working with S/4HANA On-Premise/Private Cloud OData APIs, many developers still rely on Basic Authentication for testing. While it works, OAuth 2.0 is the recommended and more secure approach.

In this blog post, I'll walk you through the complete process — from verifying your system version to making your first successful API call using OAuth 2.0.

Target audience: Basis administrators, integration developers, and functional consultants who need to configure OAuth 2.0 for OData API consumption on S/4HANA On-Premise.

Flow covered in this blog: OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials Grant — suitable for system-to-system (server-to-server) integration where no end-user interaction is required.

Prerequisites

  • Administrator access — You need authorization for transactions SOAUTH2, SICF, SU01, SMICM, and PFCG
  • Any API Client tool — Such as Postman, Bruno (Recommended for localized testing in closed networks), or cURL
  • HTTPS enabled on your S/4HANA system (OAuth 2.0 requires HTTPS; HTTP will not work)
  • Valid SSL/TLS certificate — Either a CA-signed certificate or a self-signed certificate (if self-signed, you'll need to disable SSL verification in your client tool during testing)
  • OData service(s) already activated — The target OData service (e.g., API_BUSINESS_PARTNER) must be activated in transaction /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE
  • SAP_BASIS ≥ 740 SP08 — Required minimum version for OAuth 2.0 support (verified in Step 1)

Step 1: Verify Your System Version

Before starting, confirm that your system supports OAuth 2.0.
Navigate to: System → Status (or use transaction SPAM to check component versions).

Key components to verify

Component Release SP-Level Description

SAP_BASIS7580003Must be ≥ 740 SP08 for OAuth 2.0 support
S4CORE1080003S/4HANA 2023
S4FND1080003S/4HANA Foundation


S4CORE to S/4HANA version mapping

S4CORE S/4HANA Version

104S/4HANA 2019
105S/4HANA 2020
106S/4HANA 2021
107S/4HANA 2022
108S/4HANA 2023
109S/4HANA 2024

If SAP_BASIS is 740 SP08 or higher, you're good to go with OAuth 2.0. ✓

Step 2: Identify Your HTTP(S) URL and Available APIs

2-1. Find Your System's HTTPS Endpoint

Transaction: SMICM → Menu → Goto → Services

Protocol Port Status

HTTPS443XXActive ✓

Your base URL becomes:

https://<hostname>:<port>

Example:
  https://gclp90XX.devxx.net.sap:443XX

⚠️OAuth 2.0 requires HTTPS. If only HTTP is active, you must configure HTTPS first. This involves:

  • Setting up an SSL server certificate via transaction STRUST (SSL Server Standard)
  • Configuring the HTTPS port in transaction SMICM (or via profile parameter icm/server_port_<xx>)
  • Restarting the ICM after changes

2-2. Verify OData Service Availability

Before configuring OAuth, make sure your target OData service is properly activated and accessible.

Check the service catalog:

https://<host>:<port>/sap/opu/odata/IWFND/CATALOGSERVICE;v=2/ServiceCollection?$format=json

If you get a JSON response with a list of services, your OData infrastructure is working. ✓

Check specific API metadata:

https://<host>:<port>/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_BUSINESS_PARTNER/$metadata

If the service is not listed, you need to activate it:

  1. Go to transaction /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE
  2. Click "Add Service"
  3. Specify the System Alias (e.g., LOCAL) and search for the technical service name (e.g., API_BUSINESS_PARTNER)
  4. Select and activate the service

⚠️The OData service must be activated here before it can be assigned as a scope in SOAUTH2.

Step 3: Configure OAuth 2.0 on S/4HANA

This is the core configuration step. We need to: activate ICF services for OAuth endpoints, create a communication user, assign proper authorization roles, register the OAuth 2.0 client, and configure scopes.

3-1. Activate ICF Services

Transaction: SICF

Make sure the following services are activated:

ICF Path Purpose

/sap/bc/sec/oauth2/tokenToken endpoint — issues access tokens
/sap/bc/sec/oauth2/authorizeAuthorization endpoint (needed for Authorization Code flow; good practice to activate)
/sap/opu/odata/OData service root — must also be active for API calls

How to activate: Navigate to the path in the tree structure → Right-click → "Activate Service"

⚠️If these services are not activated, token requests will return HTTP 404 or HTTP 500 errors.

3-2. Prepare a Communication User

Transaction: SU01

In S/4HANA OAuth 2.0 (Client Credentials flow), the Client ID is the User ID and the Client Secret is the User's Password.

Create or configure the user as follows:

Field Value Notes

User IDTEST_CLIENTThis becomes your OAuth Client ID
User TypeSystem or CommunicationDo NOT use Dialog type for system-to-system integration. Communication type (type "C") is recommended for S/4HANA.
Password(your chosen password)This becomes your OAuth Client Secret. "New Password" and "Repeat Password" must match exactly.

⚠️Common error: "The passwords specified must be identical" — Ensure "New Password" and "Repeat Password" match exactly. Watch for trailing spaces or IME input issues.

3-3. Assign Authorization Roles to the Communication User

Transaction: PFCG (to create/modify roles) and SU01 (to assign roles to user)

The communication user must have proper authorization to access the target OData service. Without this, even a valid token will result in HTTP 403 Forbidden errors.

Required authorizations:

  • OData service authorization: Authorization object S_SERVICE with:
    • SRV_NAME = Technical service name (e.g., API_BUSINESS_PARTNER — check the exact value in /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE)
    • SRV_TYPE = HT (HTTP Service)
  • OAuth scope authorization: Authorization object S_SCOPE with:
    • S_SCOPE = The scope ID that you will configure in SOAUTH2 (e.g., ZAPI_BUSINESS_PARTNER_0001)
  • Business data authorization: Depending on the API, additional authorization objects may be required (e.g., F_BKPF_BUK for accounting documents, B_BUPA_GRP for Business Partner, etc.)

Quick approach for testing: You can assign the role SAP_BC_WEBSERVICE_CONSUMER (or create a custom role with the necessary S_SERVICE entries) and assign it to TEST_CLIENT in SU01 → Roles tab.

⚠️Important: After assigning roles, perform a user comparison in PFCG (User tab → User Comparison) to ensure the authorization profile is generated and up to date.

3-4. Register OAuth 2.0 Client

Transaction: SOAUTH2

  1. Click "Create"
  2. OAuth 2.0 Client ID: TEST_CLIENT
    ⚠️This must exactly match your SU01 User ID — the system maps the OAuth client to this user account.
  3. Client Authentication: Ensure "Client ID and Secret" is selected (this is the default and required for Client Credentials flow).
  4. Grant Types: ☑ Client Credentials
    Leave other grant types unchecked unless needed.

・Create OAuth 2.0 Client

・Client Authentication

・Grant Type Settings — Check "Grant Type Client Credentials.."

・Configure Scopes 

Scopes define which OData services the OAuth client is allowed to access. Without a correctly configured scope, token requests will fail with invalid_scope.

How to add a scope:

  1. In the "Scope" tab, click "Add"
  2. The system shows a list of available OAuth 2.0 scopes derived from active ICF/OData services
  3. Search for and select the scope corresponding to your target OData service
  4. Note the exact Scope ID displayed — you will need this exact string when requesting the token

Understanding Scope IDs:

The Scope ID is typically auto-generated based on the OData service's ICF node path. It often follows a pattern like:

ZAPI_BUSINESS_PARTNER_0001

or in some systems:

/SAP/OPU/ODATA/SAP/API_BUSINESS_PARTNER_0001

⚠️The scope value you send in the token request must match the Scope ID in SOAUTH2 exactly — including case, underscores, slashes, and any suffixes like _0001.

If no scopes are available in the list:

  • The OData service may not be activated in /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE
  • The ICF node for the OData service may not be active in SICF
  • The OAuth 2.0 scope may need to be defined. Check SAP Note 3023072 and 2769057 for scope configuration details.

Verifying scope configuration:

  • You can also check available scopes via the token endpoint by intentionally sending an incorrect scope — the error response sometimes hints at valid scopes
  • Transaction SOAUTH2 → double-click on the client → Scope tab clearly shows all assigned scopes and their exact IDs

Step 4: Configure API Client Tool and Get Access Token

4-1. Get Access Token (Client Credentials Flow)

In your API client tool (Bruno, Postman, etc.), configure the OAuth 2.0 settings:

Using Bruno: Go to the "Auth" tab of your request:

Setting Value

TypeOAuth 2.0
Grant TypeClient Credentials
Access Token URLhttps://<host>:<port>/sap/bc/sec/oauth2/token
Client IDTEST_CLIENT
Client Secret<your_SU01_User_password>
Scopeex. ZAPI_BUSINESS_PARTNER_0001 (Must match SOAUTH2 Scope ID exactly)
Client AuthenticationSend as Basic Auth header

Click "Get Access Token"

→ If successful, you'll receive a Bearer token 🎉

Using cURL (alternative for quick testing):

curl -X POST "https://<host>:<port>/sap/bc/sec/oauth2/token" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
  -u "TEST_CLIENT:<your_password>" \
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials&scope=ZAPI_BUSINESS_PARTNER_0001" \
  -k

(The -k flag skips SSL verification for self-signed certificates during testing.)

Successful response example:

{
  "access_token": "eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIs...",
  "token_type": "Bearer",
  "expires_in": 3600,
  "scope": "ZAPI_BUSINESS_PARTNER_0001"
}

⚠️Note: The default token lifetime is typically 3600 seconds (1 hour). After expiry, you need to request a new token. The expiry can be configured in SOAUTH2.

4-2. Troubleshooting Token Errors

If you get "No access token received", use Bruno's Timeline tab or a manual POST request (e.g., cURL) to see the raw error from SAP:

Error Cause Solution

invalid_scopeThe Scope string doesn't match what's configured in SOAUTH2Open SOAUTH2 → Scope tab → copy the exact Scope ID. Check for prefixes, suffixes (_0001), slashes, and case sensitivity.
invalid_clientThe Client ID or Client Secret (password) is incorrectVerify the User ID in SU01 matches the Client ID in SOAUTH2. Reset the password if needed. Check that the user is not locked.
unauthorized_clientThe grant type is not enabled for this clientCheck SOAUTH2 → Grant Type tab → ensure "Client Credentials" is checked.
HTTP 404Token endpoint ICF service is not activatedActivate /sap/bc/sec/oauth2/token in SICF.
HTTP 500Internal server error — often SSL or configuration issueCheck SMICM → Trace File for details. Also check ST22 for ABAP dumps.
SSL/TLS handshake errorCertificate issueIf using self-signed cert: disable SSL verification in your client tool. For Bruno: Settings → General → SSL/TLS Certificate Verification → OFF.
User lockedToo many failed password attemptsUnlock the user in SU01 → Lock/Unlock tab, or use SU10 for mass unlock.

Additional diagnostics:

  • Transaction SMICM → Goto → Trace File → view HTTP request/response details
  • Transaction ST22 → check for ABAP runtime errors (dumps)
  • Transaction SU53 → check authorization failures for the communication user (log in as that user or check after a failed request)

Step 5: Make Your First API Call

5-1. GET Request (Read Business Partner)

GET https://<host>:<port>/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_BUSINESS_PARTNER/A_BusinessPartner?$top=5&$format=json

Headers:
  Authorization: Bearer {{your_access_token}}

Expected response: 200 OK with Business Partner data in JSON ✓

Common errors at this stage:

HTTP Status Cause Solution

401 UnauthorizedToken expired or invalidRequest a new access token
403 ForbiddenUser lacks authorization for the OData service or underlying business dataCheck roles assigned in SU01. Use SU53 to identify missing authorizations. Add required authorization objects to the user's role in PFCG.
404 Not FoundOData service path is incorrect or service is not activatedVerify the URL path. Check /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE for service activation.

Useful Transactions Reference

Transaction Purpose

SMICMCheck HTTP/HTTPS ports, ICM status, and trace logs
SOAUTH2OAuth 2.0 client registration, grant types, and scope configuration
SICFActivate / deactivate ICF services (including OAuth endpoints)
SU01User management — create communication user and set Client Secret (password)
PFCGRole maintenance — create/assign authorization roles with S_SERVICE, S_SCOPE, etc.
SU53Authorization check analysis — identify missing authorizations after a failed request
STRUSTSSL certificate management — configure server and client certificates
/IWFND/MAINT_SERVICEOData service activation and management on the SAP Gateway hub
ST22ABAP dump analysis — check for runtime errors during token or API requests

Summary of Configuration Steps

Here's a quick checklist to ensure nothing is missed:

  1. ✅System version check — SAP_BASIS ≥ 740 SP08
  2. ✅HTTPS is active — Verified in SMICM
  3. ✅OData service is activated — Verified in /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE
  4. ✅ICF services activated — /sap/bc/sec/oauth2/token and /sap/bc/sec/oauth2/authorize in SICF
  5. ✅Communication user created — User type System/Communication in SU01
  6. ✅Authorization roles assigned — S_SERVICE, S_SCOPE, and business authorizations via PFCG
  7. ✅OAuth 2.0 client registered — Client ID matches User ID in SOAUTH2
  8. ✅Grant type enabled — Client Credentials checked in SOAUTH2
  9. ✅Scope configured — Correct scope added and Scope ID noted in SOAUTH2
  10. ✅Token request successful — Bearer token received
  11. ✅API call successful — HTTP 200 OK with data returned

Conclusion

Setting up OAuth 2.0 for S/4HANA OData APIs involves several configuration steps, but once done, it provides a much more secure and standards-based authentication mechanism compared to Basic Authentication.

The most common pitfalls are:

  • Scope mismatch — Always copy the exact Scope ID from SOAUTH2
  • Missing authorization roles — The communication user needs both OAuth-related and business-data authorizations
  • ICF services not activated — Both the OAuth token endpoint and the OData service endpoints must be active

I hope this guide helps you get started with OAuth 2.0 on your S/4HANA landscape!
Shusei Sekiya

entry.title: Efficient Delta Creation in the Datasphere: A Practical Guide
entry.content:
1                Introduction: The Importance of Delta Processing in Modern Data Architectures
Nowadays, large amounts of data are transported from A to B. Therefore, it is becoming increasingly important to either consume or analyze data virtually, or to consume a delta. Since this is not always possible, this article will show how I have solved this problem within the Datasphere.

Step 1: Extracting Data into a Staging Table
The first step is straightforward: extract the data from the source system into a staging table.

At this stage, the goal is not to transform the data but simply to create a 1:1 copy of the source dataset via Replication Flow. This staging layer represents the most recent snapshdot of the source system. 

The staging table acts as the input layer for delta processing. Each load refreshes this table with the latest data extracted from the source.

Step 2: Creating a Hash for Change Detection
To detect changes efficiently, we generate a hash value for each record based on the relevant business attributes.

The idea behind this is simple: If any field changes, the hash value changes as well.

This avoids expensive field-by-field comparisons and keeps the comparison logic compact.

Example for SQL-View for new data:

SELECT
    MATERIAL,
    MATERIAL_DESC,
    QUANTITY,
    HASH_SHA256(
        TO_BINARY(
            MATERIAL || MATERIAL_DESC || QUANTITY
        )
    ) AS HASH_VALUE,
    LOAD_TS
FROM IL_LT_MATERIAL

This hash essentially acts as a fingerprint of the record. When we compare two datasets later, we only need to compare the hash values instead of every column.

For large datasets, this approach can significantly simplify the change detection process.

Beside the new replicated Data we have the "active" Data in another table, which are loaded before. The hash-value is created as well, but over an Transformation Flow. After that we can compare each line over the key (Material).

Step 3: Calculating the Delta
Once the staging data contains the hash values, it can be compared to the current active dataset.

This comparison identifies three possible scenarios:

  • INSERT – the record exists in staging but not in the active dataset
  • UPDATE – the record exists in both datasets but the hash value changed
  • DELETE – the record exists in the active dataset but not in the new staging data

The first time the pipeline runs (initial load), there is no previous dataset to compare against. In that case, the active dataset is simply initialized.

From the second run onward, the system starts identifying real deltas.

Example logic for SQL-View:

SELECT
N.MATERIAL,
N.MATERIAL_DESC,
N.QUANTITY,
CURRENT_TIMESTAMP AS CHANGE_TS,
CASE
WHEN A.MATERIAL IS NULL THEN 'I'
WHEN N.HASH_VALUE <> A.HASH_VALUE THEN 'U'
WHEN N.MATERIAL IS NULL THEN 'DELETE'
END AS DELTA_TYPE
FROM IL_SV_MATERIAL_HASH N
FULL OUTER JOIN IT_LT_MATERIAL_ACTIVE A
ON S.MATERIAL = A.MATERIAL;

Each detected change is stored together with a timestamp, which allows us to track when the change was detected.

Material_DeltaTable

This timestamp becomes particularly useful for auditing, monitoring, and time-based queries.

Step 4: Maintaining the Delta Table
The detected changes are written into a delta table. Instead of storing the full dataset again, this table only contains the records that changed.

Typical columns include:

  • Business key (e.g. MATERIAL, PLANT)
  • Updated values
  • Change type (INSERT / UPDATE / DELETE)
  • Change timestamp

With each new load, additional delta records are appended.

Over time, the table becomes a history of detected changes, which can be extremely helpful for troubleshooting or reconstructing data states.

Step 5: Providing the Delta to Data Consumers
Once the delta table is maintained, it can be exposed to downstream consumers.

Via Replication Flow the Data can be provided to the target, for e.g. ADLS (Premium Outbound neccessary)

Applications, dashboards, or integration services can query the delta table and filter by change type.

For example:

  • retrieve only new records
  • process updates
  • synchronize deletions

Because only the changed data is provided, consumers do not need to process the entire dataset each time.

This keeps integrations lightweight and improves overall system performance.

Process Overview
The complete workflow can be summarized in five steps:

  1. Extract the latest data into a staging table
  2. Generate a hash value for each record
  3. Compare the staging dataset with the active dataset
  4. Identify inserts, updates, and deletes
  5. Store and expose the delta for downstream consumption
